1S/C18H23NO3/c1-13(2-3-14-4-7-16(20)8-5-14)19-11-10-15-6-9-17(21)18(22)12-15/h4-9,12-13,19-22H,2-3,10-11H2,1H3 | StdInChIKey_Ref = {{stdinchicite|correct|chemspider}} | StdInChIKey_Ref = {{stdinchicite|correct|chemspider}} | StdInChIKey = JRWZLRBJNMZMFE-UHFFFAOYSA-N | StdInChIKey = JRWZLRBJNMZMFE-UHFFFAOYSA-N | synonyms = Dobutrex, Inotrex }} }} <!-- Definition and medical uses --> ⚫ '''Dobutamine''' is a [[sympathomimetic]] [[drug]] used in the treatment of [[heart failure]] and [[cardiogenic shock]]. Its primary mechanism is direct stimulation of [[beta receptor|β<sub>1</sub> receptors]] of the [[sympathetic nervous system]]. Dobutamine was developed by a laboratory led by Drs. Ronald Tuttle and Jack Mills at [[Eli Lilly and Company]], as a [[analog (chemistry)|structural analogue]] of [[isoprenaline]].<ref>{{cite journal | author =Tuttle RR, Mills J |title=Dobutamine: development of a new catecholamine to selectively increase cardiac contractility |journal= Circ Res |volume=36 |issue=1 |pages= 185–96 | year =1975 | month=January | pmid=234805 | url=http://circres.ahajournals.org/cgi/reprint/36/1/185 | doi=10.1161/01.RES.36.1.185}}</ref> '''Dobutamine''' is a medication used in the treatment of [[cardiogenic shock]] (as a result of inadequate tissue perfusion) and severe [[heart failure]].<ref name=AHFS2019/><ref name=Wil2007/> It may also be used in certain types of [[cardiac stress tests]].<ref name=AHFS2019/> It is given by IV only, as an [[intravenous therapy|injection into a vein]] or [[intraosseous infusion|intraosseous]] as a [[continuous infusion]].<ref name=AHFS2019/> The amount of medication needs to be adjusted to the desired effect.<ref name=AHFS2019/> Onset of effects is generally seen within 2 minutes.<ref name=AHFS2019/> It has a half-life of two minutes. This drug is generally only administered short term, although it may be used for longer periods to relieve symptoms of [[heart failure]] in patients awaiting heart transplantation.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Gentile P, Marini C, Ammirati E, Perna E, Saponara G, Garascia A, D'Angelo L, Verde A, Foti G, Masciocco G, Frigerio M, Cipriani M | display-authors = 6 | title = Long-term administration of intravenous inotropes in advanced heart failure | journal = ESC Heart Failure | volume = 8 | issue = 5 | pages = 4322–4327 | date = October 2021 | pmid = 34191408 | pmc = 8497373 | doi = 10.1002/ehf2.13394 }}</ref> <!-- Side effects and mechanism --> ⚫ == Clinical uses== Common side effects include a [[tachycardia|fast heart rate]], an [[irregular heart beat]], and inflammation at the site of injection.<ref name=AHFS2019>{{cite web |title=Dobutamine Hydrochloride Monograph for Professionals |url=https://www.drugs.com/monograph/dobutamine-hydrochloride.html |website=Drugs.com |publisher=AHFS |access-date=11 January 2019 }}</ref><ref name=BNF76/> Use is not recommended in those with [[idiopathic hypertrophic subaortic stenosis]].<ref name=AHFS2019/> It primarily works by direct stimulation of [[beta receptor|β<sub>1</sub> receptors]], which increases the strength of the [[heart]]'s contractions, leading to a positive inotropic effect. Generally it has little effect on a person's [[heart rate]].<ref name=AHFS2019/> ⚫ Dobutamine is used to treat acute but potentially reversible [[heart failure]], such as which occurs during [[cardiac surgery]] or in cases of [[Septic shock|septic]] or cardiogenic shock, on the basis of its positive [[inotrope|inotropic]] action.<ref name="rang">{{cite book | author =Rang HP, Dale MM, Ritter JM, Flower RJ |title=Rang and Dale's Pharmacology}}</ref> <!-- History and culture --> ⚫ Dobutamine can be used in cases of [[congestive heart failure]] to increase cardiac output. It is indicated when [[Total parenteral nutrition|parenteral therapy]] is necessary for inotropic support in the short-term treatment of patients with cardiac [[decompensation]] due to depressed [[contractility]], which could be the result of either organic heart disease or cardiac surgical procedures. It is not useful in [[ischemic heart disease]] because it increases heart rate and thus increases myocardial oxygen demand. Dobutamine was approved for medical use in the United States in 1978.<ref name=AHFS2019/> It is available as a [[generic medication]].<ref name=BNF76>{{cite book|title=British national formulary : BNF 76|date=2018|publisher=Pharmaceutical Press|isbn=978-0-85711-338-2 |pages=220–221|edition=76}}</ref> It was initially made from [[isoproterenol]].<ref name=Wil2007>{{cite book | vauthors = Wilson WC, Grande CM, Hoyt DB |title=Trauma: Critical Care |date=2007 |publisher=CRC Press |isbn=978-1-4200-1684-0 |page=302 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3H3AIEtvc8YC&pg=PA302 }}</ref> ⚫ == Medical uses== ⚫ Dobutamine is used to treat acute but potentially reversible [[heart failure]], such as which occurs during [[cardiac surgery]] or in cases of [[Septic shock|septic]] or cardiogenic shock, on the basis of its positive [[inotrope|inotropic]] action.<ref name="rang">{{cite book | vauthors =Rang HP, Dale MM, Ritter JM, Flower RJ |title=Rang and Dale's Pharmacology}}</ref> ⚫ Dobutamine can be used in cases of [[congestive heart failure]] to increase cardiac output. It is indicated when [[Total parenteral nutrition|parenteral therapy]] is necessary for inotropic support in the short-term treatment of patients with cardiac [[decompensation]] due to depressed [[ Myocardial contractility| contractility]], which could be the result of either organic heart disease or cardiac surgical procedures. Higher doses are not useful with history of recent [[ischemic heart disease]] because it increases heart rate and thus increases myocardial oxygen demand. <ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Tariq S, Aronow WS | title = Use of Inotropic Agents in Treatment of Systolic Heart Failure | journal = International Journal of Molecular Sciences | volume = 16 | issue = 12 | pages = 29060–29068 | date = December 2015 | pmid = 26690127 | pmc = 4691094 | doi = 10.3390/ijms161226147 | doi-access = free }}</ref> The drug is also commonly used in the hospital setting as a pharmacologic stress testing agent to identify coronary artery disease. ==Adverse effects== ==Adverse effects== Primary side effects include those commonly seen for β<sub>1</sub> active sympathomimetics, such as [[hypertension]], [[angina]], [[arrhythmia]], and [[tachycardia]]. Used with caution in atrial fibrillation as it has the effect of increasing the atriovenrticular (AV) conduction.<ref name=pharmnemonics>{{Cite book | author =Shen , Howard | title=Illustrated Pharmacology Memory Cards: PharMnemonics | year=2008 | publisher=Minireview | isbn=1-59541-101- 1 | page=6}}</ref> Primary side effects include those commonly seen for β<sub>1</sub> active sympathomimetics, such as [[hypertension]], [[angina]], [[ Heart arrhythmia| arrhythmia]], and [[tachycardia]]. Used with caution in atrial fibrillation as it has the effect of increasing the atrioventricular (AV) conduction.<ref name=pharmnemonics>{{Cite book | vauthors = Shen H | title=Illustrated Pharmacology Memory Cards: PharMnemonics | year=2008 | publisher=Minireview | isbn= 978- 1-59541-101- 3 | page=6}}</ref> The most dangerous side effect of dobutamine is increased risk of arrhythmia, including fatal arrhythmias . Studies suggest that while this medication can improve symptoms in chronic [[Heart failure|CHF]], it actually shortens a patient's lifespan . The most dangerous side effect of dobutamine is increased risk of arrhythmia, including fatal arrhythmias. Overall, dobutamine tends to produce less tachycardia and peripheral vascular effects than agents such as epinephrine and isoproterenol. ==Pharmacology== ==Pharmacology== Dobutamine is a direct-acting agent whose primary activity results from stimulation of the [[beta-1 adrenoceptor|β<sub>1</sub>-adrenoceptors]] of the heart, increasing contractility and cardiac output. Since it does not act on [[dopamine receptor]]s to induce the release of [[norepinephrine]] (another α<sub>1</sub> agonist), dobutamine is less prone to induce hypertension than is [[dopamine]]. Dobutamine is a direct-acting agent whose primary activity results from stimulation of the [[beta-1 adrenoceptor|β<sub>1</sub>-adrenoceptors]] of the heart, increasing contractility and cardiac output. Since it does not act on [[dopamine receptor]]s to inhibit the release of [[norepinephrine]] (another α<sub>1</sub> agonist), dobutamine is less prone to induce hypertension than is [[dopamine]]. Dobutamine is predominantly a [[beta-1 agonist|β<sub>1</sub>-adrenergic agonist]], with weak β<sub>2</sub> activity, and [[alpha-1 receptor|α<sub>1</sub>]] selective activity, although it is used clinically in cases of cardiogenic shock for its β<sub>1</sub> [[inotropic]] effect in increasing heart contractility and cardiac output. Dobutamine is administered as a [[racemic mixture]] consisting of both (+) and (−) [[chirality (chemistry)|isomer]]s; the (+) isomer is a potent β<sub>1</sub> agonist and α<sub>1</sub> antagonist, while the (−) isomer is an α<sub>1</sub> agonist.<ref>{{cite book | author =Parker K, Brunton L, Goodman LS, Blumenthal D, Buxton I |title=Goodman & Gilman's manual of pharmacology and therapeutics |publisher=McGraw-Hill Medical |location= |year=2008 |pages=159 |isbn=0-07-144343- 6 }}</ref> The administration of the racemate results in the overall β<sub>1</sub> agonism responsible for its activity. (+)-Dobutamine also has mild β<sub>2</sub> agonist activity, which makes it useful as a vasodilator.<ref>{{cite journal | author =Tibayan FA, Chesnutt AN, Folkesson HG, Eandi J, Matthay MA |title=Dobutamine increases alveolar liquid clearance in ventilated rats by beta-2 receptor stimulation |journal= Am. J. Respir. Crit. Care Med. |volume=156 |issue=2 Pt 1 |pages= 438–44 | year =1997 |pmid=9279221}}</ref> Dobutamine is predominantly a [[beta-1 agonist|β<sub>1</sub>-adrenergic agonist]], with weak β<sub>2</sub> activity, and [[alpha-1 receptor|α<sub>1</sub>]] selective activity, although it is used clinically in cases of cardiogenic shock for its β<sub>1</sub> [[inotropic]] effect in increasing heart contractility and cardiac output. Dobutamine is administered as a [[racemic mixture]] consisting of both (+) and (−) [[chirality (chemistry)|isomer]]s; the (+) isomer is a potent β<sub>1</sub> agonist and α<sub>1</sub> antagonist, while the (−) isomer is an α<sub>1</sub> agonist.<ref>{{cite book | vauthors =Parker K, Brunton L, Goodman LS, Blumenthal D, Buxton I |title=Goodman & Gilman's manual of pharmacology and therapeutics |url=https://archive.org/details/goodmangilmansma00brun |url-access=limited |publisher=McGraw-Hill Medical |year=2008 |pages= [https://archive.org/details/goodmangilmansma00brun/page/n169 159 ] |isbn= 978- 0-07-144343- 2 }}</ref> The administration of the racemate results in the overall β<sub>1</sub> agonism responsible for its activity. (+)-Dobutamine also has mild β<sub>2</sub> agonist activity, which makes it useful as a vasodilator.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Tibayan FA, Chesnutt AN, Folkesson HG, Eandi J, Matthay MA | title = Dobutamine increases alveolar liquid clearance in ventilated rats by beta-2 receptor stimulation | journal = American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine | volume = 156 | issue = 2 Pt 1 | pages = 438–444 | date = August 1997 | pmid = 9279221 | doi = 10.1164/ajrccm.156.2.9609141 }}</ref> ==Chemistry== It is synthesized by the reaction of 2-(3,4-dimethoxyphenyl)ethanamine and 1-(4-methoxyphenyl)-3-butanone with a simultaneous reduction of formed [[imine]]. The methoxyl-protecting groups of this intermediate are cleaved by [[hydrogen bromide]] giving dobutamine. [[File:Dobutamine synthesis.png|800px]] *R. R. Tuttle, J. Mills, {{Cite patent|DE|2317710}} (1973). *J. Mills, R. R. Tuttle, {{US Patent|3987200}} (1976). == References == == History == ⚫ It was developed in the 1970s by Drs. Ronald Tuttle and Jack Mills at [[Eli Lilly and Company]], as a [[analog (chemistry)|structural analogue]] of [[isoprenaline]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Tuttle RR, Mills J | title = Dobutamine: development of a new catecholamine to selectively increase cardiac contractility | journal = Circulation Research | volume = 36 | issue = 1 | pages = 185–196 | date = January 1975 | pmid = 234805 | doi = 10.1161/01.RES.36.1.185 | doi-access = free }}</ref> {{reflist}} == References == ⚫ {{Adrenergic agonists }} {{Reflist}} ⚫ {{Cardiac stimulants excluding cardiac glycosides}} == External links == ⚫ [[Category: Beta -adrenergic agonists]] * {{cite web | url = https://druginfo.nlm.nih.gov/drugportal/name/dobutamine | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20190628213804/https://druginfo.nlm.nih.gov/drugportal/name/dobutamine | url-status = dead | archive-date = June 28, 2019 | publisher = U.S. National Library of Medicine | work = Drug Information Portal | title = Dobutamine }} ⚫ [[Category: ⚫ {{Cardiac stimulants excluding cardiac glycosides}} ⚫ {{Adrenergic receptor modulators }} {{Phenethylamines}} {{Portal bar | Medicine}} [[Category:Antihypotensive agents]] [[az:Dobutamin]] ⚫ [[Category: Beta1 -adrenergic agonists]] [[de:Dobutamin]] ⚫ [[Category: Catecholamines]] [[es:Dobutamina]] [[Category:Drugs developed by Eli Lilly and Company]] [[hr:Dobutamin]] [[Category:4-Hydroxyphenyl compounds]] [[it:Dobutamina]] [[Category:Inotropic agents]] [[hu:Dobutamin]] [[Category:Wikipedia medicine articles ready to translate]] [[ja:ドブタミン]] [[pl:Dobutamina]] [[pt:Dobutamina]] [[ru:Добутамин]] [[uk:Добутамін]] Retrieved from " https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special:ComparePages "
